summary: Ronacher announces that Rye will move under Astral’s stewardship and now runs on top of uv, a faster replacement for pip-tools and venv. He frames Rye as an experimental test bed for what “cargo for Python” could look like, noting both the promise and the brittleness of its current design. The speed and UX gains from uv and Astral’s tooling (like ruff) make the direction feel inevitable. The plan is to keep Rye as a breeding ground for ideas while uv evolves from low-level plumbing into a higher-level, unified tool. He will stay involved, but emphasizes that solving these ecosystem problems is bigger than a single maintainer.
key_points: Rye is shifting to Astral’s stewardship and now layers on uv for much faster package management.
Rye’s purpose is experimentation: probing what a “cargo for Python” might be and exposing ecosystem gaps.
Astral’s tools (uv, ruff) already power parts of Rye, shaping a path toward convergence.
Rye is intentionally not the final solution; its current architecture is brittle and exploratory.
The long-term vision is a migration path from rye to a higher-level uv tool.
money_quotes: For me Rye is an exciting test bed of what Python tooling can be.
I wanted to explore what a “cargo for Python” is like and it's becoming ever more evident what that might look like.
Instead of taking 5 seconds to sync a virtualenv, it's almost instant.
It's hard to overstate how impactful this is in terms of developer experience.
Rye will turn into a blessed breeding ground of different things.
As the user experience becomes more obvious uv itself will turn from what it is today — low level plumbing — into that higher level tool.
Rye as it exists today is unlikely to be the final solution.
key_insight: Rye is being repositioned as an experimental incubator so uv can grow into the eventual “cargo for Python,” backed by Astral’s stewardship and tooling speedups.
